title: Process financial documents using Amazon Bedrock Data Automation
summary: In this post, we explore how Amazon Bedrock Data Automation can accurately extract information from four common types of financial documents: bank statements, W-2 forms, 1099-B tax forms, and vendor contracts. We highlight the complexity in the documents, detail the custom extraction created in Amazon Bedrock Data Automation, and describe the outcomes of the extraction process.

processed.summary:
Introduces **Amazon Bedrock Data Automation** as a managed service to automate information extraction from complex, semi-structured, and unstructured financial documents.
Employs **Generative AI foundation models** to bypass traditional template-based OCR, enabling context-aware data retrieval from bank statements and tax forms.
Features **custom extraction schemas** that allow ML engineers to define specific fields of interest without the need for extensive manual data labeling or model fine-tuning.
Optimizes **enterprise workflows** by providing a scalable orchestration layer that handles document layout analysis and data normalization natively within the AWS ecosystem.
key_results: Automated extraction from bank statements, W-2 forms, 1099-B forms, and vendor contracts.
Elimination of manual rule-based configuration for varying document layouts.
Support for multi-page, dense financial documents with high tabular accuracy.
Standardization of extracted data into structured formats for downstream analysis.
Reduction in development time for building production-grade Document AI pipelines.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Amazon Web Services launches Bedrock Data Automation to streamline high-accuracy information extraction from complex, unstructured financial documents.

title: How AWS SMGS uses an AI-powered conversational assistant to transform business management with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
summary: In this post, we share how we built NarrateAI using Amazon Bedrock AgentCore to deliver business intelligence at scale for the AWS SMGS (Sales, Marketing and Global Services) organization. processed.summary:

* The system implements robust **engineering patterns** for production, focusing on the reliability of business intelligence delivery within the AWS Sales and Marketing ecosystem.
* It provides a framework for scaling **generative AI assistants** by using a modular approach to agent responsibilities and data governance.
key_results: Development of NarrateAI for global business intelligence scaling.
Decoupling of data ingestion from user-facing conversation logic.
Implementation of specialized routing agents to improve query accuracy.
Establishment of validation layers to ensure data integrity in responses.

title: Go: Support for Generic Methods

processed.summary:
The proposal seeks to introduce **generic methods** to the Go language, allowing methods to define their own **type parameters** separate from the receiver type.
It addresses a long-standing limitation in Go's **generics implementation** which currently restricts type parameters to functions and type definitions.
The architectural impact involves significant changes to **interface satisfaction** and how the compiler handles dynamic dispatch for methods with independent type parameters.
key_results: Introduction of method-level type parameters for increased API flexibility.
Resolution of the 'generic method' limitation present since Go 1.18.
Potential impact on interface implementation and runtime performance.
Proposed syntax remains consistent with existing Go generics design.
Addresses complex edge cases involving reflection and dynamic linking.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The Go team proposes adding generic methods to resolve architectural constraints and enhance flexibility within the language's type system.

title: JWT is a scam and your app doesn't need it

processed.summary:
• Challenges the common industry practice of using **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** for session management, arguing that it introduces unnecessary complexity and security risks compared to traditional cookies.
• Critiques the **statelessness** argument, noting that any requirement for token revocation (e.g., logout or account banning) forces a stateful database check, which negates the primary architectural benefit of JWTs.
• Recommends the use of **opaque session tokens** stored in HttpOnly, Secure cookies to prevent XSS-based token theft and simplify server-side authentication logic.
key_results: JWTs are frequently used for session management where stateful cookies would be more secure.
True statelessness is rarely achieved because session revocation requires a central blacklist or database.
Storing JWTs in LocalStorage exposes applications to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks.
Opaque tokens minimize the attack surface by keeping session data strictly server-side.
JWTs should be restricted to specific use cases like short-lived tokens for cross-service communication.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Dusan Malusev argues that developers should replace JWTs with opaque session cookies to improve security and reduce architectural complexity.

Automated content generation often bypasses basic quality assurance layers.
